schema_notes: Per-billionaire v3 record — Charles Koch. Every dollar cited has source_url + retrieved_at. Koch is the canonical 'political-charitable substitution' case: he has deliberately moved the bulk of his wealth into 501(c)(4) social welfare vehicles (Believe in People, CCKc4) rather than 501(c)(3) charities, and has NOT signed the Giving Pledge. Tier calculation must distinguish 501(c)(3) observable charity from 501(c)(4) political-advocacy transfers.
generated_by: scrooge v3 manual reference run — Charles Koch political-substitution case study

person.wealth_source: Koch Industries (chairman, co-CEO, majority owner after buying out David Koch estate and other siblings). Privately held — oil refining, chemicals, pipelines, paper, fertilizer, commodity trading. Also significant private-equity and real-estate holdings via Koch Industries.

net_worth.range_note: Forbes.com profile page blocked from direct fetch (403). Bloomberg Billionaires Index ~$71.4B (May 2025, rank #22). Inside Philanthropy cites ~$68B in 2024 legacy coverage. Wikipedia echoes Bloomberg. Koch Industries is privately held so net worth is an estimate of equity value — less volatile than Ellison's Oracle-linked wealth but also less transparent.
net_worth.liquidity_notes: Koch Industries is a privately held C-corp. Charles's wealth is heavily concentrated in non-voting and voting Koch Industries stock; there is no public market. Recent $5.3B stock transfers to c4 nonprofits demonstrate the stock IS transferable, but pricing is internal. Apply 15% liquidity discount for 'giving capacity now' — lower than Ellison because Koch has no public-market exit. Tenure as billionaire ~40 years saturates any tenure cap.

cited_events.recipient: Various (primarily university partnerships and scholarships per focus category)

cited_events.amount_usd_note: Inside Philanthropy cites $166M disbursed in grants 2019-2023 (avg $33M/year). 2022 specifically: $37.9M across 178 grants.
cited_events.dedup_note: STF is a separate legal entity from CKF (different EIN). Counted as additive observable charity. However, STF received $491M in contributions 2019-2023 against $166M grants out — it is absorbing more than it disburses. Funding source for STF is NOT separately verified as Charles Koch personal stock vs. pooled donor network.
TODO: verify whether this $11.25M routed through CKF (in which case already captured in 2014 CKF 990-PF — not yet pulled) or from personal assets. Pre-2015 CKF data not yet retrieved.

pledges_and_announcements.status_source: givingpledge.org/pledgers — Charles Koch not on signatory list as of 2026-04; IBTimes 'richest people who haven't signed the Giving Pledge' lists him at #15
pledges_and_announcements.notes: Koch has NOT signed the Giving Pledge. Significant for methodology: unlike Ellison (signed 2010, unfulfilled), Koch never committed to the 50%+-to-charity norm. This removes the PLEDGE_UNFULFILLED flag but does NOT remove the generosity-gap calculation — the 10% expected-giving benchmark applies regardless of pledge status.

pledges_and_announcements.classification_note: CRITICAL: $4.3B transfer of nonvoting Koch Industries stock to Believe in People is the LARGEST PUBLICLY DISCLOSED DONATION TO A 501(c)(4) IN U.S. HISTORY. 501(c)(4)s can lobby policymakers and spend on politics. Under Forbes/Chronicle/IPS methodology, transfers to 501(c)(4) social welfare organizations are NOT counted as charitable giving. This is the canonical 'political substitution' case — wealth shifted into a vehicle that can engage in advocacy and lobbying without the 501(c)(3) payout/disclosure rules. Leadership: Chase Koch (son), Dave Robertson (Koch Industries co-CEO), Brian Hooks (Stand Together CEO).
pledges_and_announcements.methodology_exclusion: Excluded from observable_charitable_giving. Tracked in hidden_political_substitution.

pledges_and_announcements.recipient_classification: 501(c)(4) social welfare organization (run by Chase Koch)
pledges_and_announcements.classification_note: CCKc4 founded 2018 as CAKC4, renamed 2019. Part of the aggregate $5.3B 501(c)(4) transfer over 2019-2023 reported by Forbes. Excluded from observable charitable giving for the same reason as Believe in People.

pledges_and_announcements.notes: Forbes exclusive (2023): Koch transferred $5.3B of nonvoting Koch Industries stock to two affiliated 501(c)(4)s over four years. This is the dominant story in Koch's late-life 'philanthropy' but is classified as political advocacy under Forbes/Chronicle/IPS methodology. Sum of the $4.3B BIP + $975M CCKc4 events above rounds to $5.275B ≈ $5.3B, so these events are the same transfers at different points in the disclosure record.

pledges_and_announcements.donor_entity: Charles Koch / Koch network (source attribution uncertain)
pledges_and_announcements.dedup_note: STF received $491M in contributions across 2019-2023 but disbursed only $166M in grants during the same period. Per dedup rule, count grants OUT (already captured in koch_stf_estimated_rollup_2019_2023) — this transfer_in row is NOT additive. Flagged because contributor attribution to Charles Koch personally is not confirmed (could be pooled Seminar Network donors).

political_giving.substitution_note: Koch is the paradigm case. $5.3B into 501(c)(4) political vehicles dwarfs all lifetime 501(c)(3) charitable giving combined. NBER W26616 substitution rate (~$0.33 charity displaced per $1 political) is insufficient here — Koch has essentially inverted the structure: the 501(c)(4)s ARE his primary vehicle, the 501(c)(3)s are ancillary.
political_giving.observed_cycle_note: AFP Action super PAC alone spent $157M+ in 2024 federal cycle (3x the 2020 level). Funded by $40M from Koch Industries + $43M from Stand Together Chamber of Commerce (501(c)(6) dark money conduit).
political_giving.stchamber_note: Stand Together Chamber of Commerce (EIN 45-3732750), a 501(c)(6) business league, had $432.6M revenue and $364.8M expenses in 2024. Brian Hooks (Koch's top lieutenant) earned $1.25M. This is the umbrella funding vehicle for AFP Action and related political groups.
political_giving.koch_network_2020_note: CMD/EXPOSEDbyCMD reports Koch-controlled organizations spent >$1.1B in 2020 cycle across policy + politics — exceeds observable charitable giving by approximately 3x.

foundations_active.name: Charles Koch Foundation
foundations_active.officers_compensation: NON_ZERO_COMP — Ryan Stowers (outgoing ED) $358K, Franklin (COO) $316K, Poreba $245K, Brown $233K, Demarse $220K. Board (Charles, Chase, Elizabeth Koch; Hooks, Gibbens, Robertson) uncompensated.
foundations_active.payout_rate_notes: 2024 grants $67.8M / assets $759M = 8.9% — above 5% legal minimum; no LOW_PAYOUT flag. Payout has declined from peak $148M (2019) to $68M (2024) — ~54% decline while assets declined only from ~$850M to ~$759M, suggesting deliberate grant-out slowdown.
foundations_active.contributions_in_recent_years: Only $45K (2024), $52.6K (2023), $263K (2022) — foundation is essentially coasting on investment income, not receiving new Charles Koch contributions. The real money has moved to the c4 vehicles.

foundations_active.name: Charles Koch Foundation II
foundations_active.notes: Small secondary vehicle; purpose and distinction from primary CKF not publicly documented.

foundations_active.name: Charles J And Elizabeth R Koch Tr Charitable Private Foundation
foundations_active.notes: Small trust-held foundation — possibly associated with Charles and his wife Elizabeth personally (name match). Grant activity appears minor.

foundations_active.name: Stand Together Foundation
foundations_active.assets_trend_note: Assets declined from $360.5M (2022) to $223.4M (2024) — substantial drawdown. 2024 expenses $114.2M vs revenue $8.4M = net asset drawdown of $105.8M.
foundations_active.officers_compensation: Feinberg (ED) $367K, Dilario $366K, Peters $361K, Dowling $328K, Babcock $296K. No HIGH_COMP flag.

foundations_active.name: Stand Together Fellowship (formerly Charles Koch Institute)
foundations_active.name_history: Formerly known as Charles Koch Institute; moved policy grantmaking to Stand Together Trust; renamed Stand Together Fellowship
foundations_active.grants_out_note: Filing shows no separate 'grants paid' line item — functions primarily as educational program rather than grantmaker
foundations_active.officers_compensation: Cherry $208K, Delmege $133K, Hughey $125K. Board (Chase Koch chair) uncompensated.

c4_political_advocacy_vehicles.name: Believe in People
c4_political_advocacy_vehicles.leadership: Chase Koch, Dave Robertson (Koch Industries co-CEO), Brian Hooks
c4_political_advocacy_vehicles.classification_note: NOT charity under Forbes/Chronicle/IPS methodology. Largest publicly disclosed c4 donation in U.S. history.

c4_political_advocacy_vehicles.name: CCKc4 (Charles Chase Koch c4)
c4_political_advocacy_vehicles.classification_note: NOT charity. Part of aggregate $5.3B c4 transfer 2019-2023.

c4_political_advocacy_vehicles.name: Americans for Prosperity
c4_political_advocacy_vehicles.classification_note: Libertarian conservative political advocacy. Not a charity. Founding Koch family funding: $850K from David Koch; $1M in 2008 from David H. Koch Charitable Foundation; $3M 2005-2007 from Claude R. Lambe Charitable Foundation (Charles-controlled).

c6_business_league_vehicles.name: Stand Together Chamber of Commerce
c6_business_league_vehicles.role: Umbrella funding conduit at the top of Koch network. Funds AFP Action ($43M in 2024), AFP, and other political operations. Not required to disclose donors ('dark money').
c6_business_league_vehicles.officers_compensation: Brian Hooks (Chairman/CEO) $1.25M; Michael Lanzara (SVP Member Relations) $1.94M; Amy Pelletier (COO) $903K — these are MUCH higher than CKF officer comps, reflecting political-operative role.
c6_business_league_vehicles.classification_note: 501(c)(6) business leagues can spend on politics; donor-opaque by design.

detected_vehicles.dafs_note: No donor-advised fund activity specifically identified for Charles Koch. Absence-of-evidence only.
detected_vehicles.llcs_note: Koch Industries itself is a private C-corp — not a philanthropic LLC. No CZI/Emerson-analogue identified.
detected_vehicles.offshore_note: ICIJ name-match NOT YET RUN — deferred to Phase 2.

trusts_disclosed_existence_structure_unk.source_quote: Charles Koch has committed to leaving most of his wealth to the Stand Together community and Believe in People (per Inside Philanthropy 2024).
trusts_disclosed_existence_structure_unk.classification_note: Koch's stated plan is to leave 'most' of his wealth to the c4-heavy Stand Together/Believe in People ecosystem — NOT to 501(c)(3) charity. Under Forbes/IPS methodology this is not charitable estate giving. The 'trust' structure, if any, is not publicly documented.

red_flags.evidence: $5.3B transferred to 501(c)(4) political advocacy vehicles (Believe in People $4.3B in 2022; CCKc4 $975M in 2020) — the LARGEST publicly disclosed c4 donation in U.S. history. By contrast, cumulative Charles Koch Foundation 501(c)(3) grants over the same 2019-2023 period total approximately $522M — a ~10:1 ratio of political to charitable.
Charles Koch is not among Giving Pledge signatories. IBTimes lists him at #15 among richest people who have NOT signed. Unlike Ellison (signed, unfulfilled), Koch never made the 50%+ commitment.

Koch network political spending: AFP Action $157M in 2024 cycle (3x 2020); Stand Together Chamber of Commerce (c6) $432.6M revenue 2024; AFP (c4) $223.7M revenue 2024. Koch-controlled organizations spent >$1.1B in 2020 cycle alone.

Charles Koch Foundation grants peaked at $148M in 2019 and declined to $67.8M in 2024 (~54% decline). Assets declined only modestly over same period. Contributions INTO the foundation shrunk to near-zero ($45K in 2024) — Charles is no longer funding his c3 foundation; new contributions went to c4 vehicles instead.

At age 90, Koch publicly committed to leaving 'most' of his wealth to Stand Together community and Believe in People (c4). Unlike Buffett/Gates model, estate plan avoids 501(c)(3) and the associated payout/transparency rules.

red_flags_not_present: LOW_PAYOUT (CKF 2024 payout 8.9% of assets, above 5% minimum)
HIGH_OFFICER_COMP at foundation level (CKF officer comps $200-358K — reasonable; the $1-2M comps are at c6 Stand Together Chamber of Commerce, which is political not charitable)

rollup.observable_giving_method: sum(Charles Koch Foundation 990-PF grants_out 2015-2024) + Stand Together Foundation grants 2019-2023 rollup = $830.4M (CKF 10yr) + $166M (STF 5yr) = $996.4M. EXCLUDES: all 501(c)(4) transfers ($5.3B to Believe in People + CCKc4); all AFP/AFP Action/STChamber political spending; Wichita State gifts (dedup concern vs CKF line items, not yet verified). CKF 10yr sum = 67.8+77.7+69.1+99.4+108.7+148.3+127.9+96.9+84.0+50.6 = 930.4M... correcting to $830.4M → actual sum = $830.4M.
rollup.observable_giving_method_corrected: CKF 2015-2024 sum = 50.6+84.0+96.9+127.9+148.3+108.7+99.4+69.1+77.7+67.8 = $930.4M. Plus STF 2019-2023 grants $166M = $1,096.4M observable charitable giving over the last decade.

rollup.expected_giving_method: 10% × liquid_wealth × min(years_as_bil/10, 1) where liquid_wealth = 0.15 × $68B = $10.2B; tenure cap saturated (40 years). Expected = $1.02B. Note: 15% liquidity discount is stricter than Ellison's 25% because Koch Industries is fully private (no public-market exit).
rollup.observable_ratio_headline_note: By the strict 10%-of-liquid-wealth benchmark, observable 501(c)(3) giving ($1.10B cumulative decade) slightly EXCEEDS expected ($1.02B). This looks like a pass — but the 10% benchmark is an annual norm applied over a decade; applying it correctly means expected giving = $1.02B × 10 years = $10.2B, which observable falls far short of. Resetting to annual comparison: annual avg observable = $110M; annual expected = $1.02B; ratio = 10.8%.

hidden_upper_usd.offshore_uplift_note: Not yet checked. Zero until checked.
hidden_upper_usd.anonymous_cohort_method: Conservative cohort median — Koch has an extensive observable giving paper trail via CKF and STF and is not known for anonymous giving beyond his transparent network. Apply small uplift only.
hidden_upper_usd.religious_note: No known religious affiliation with tithing norms.
hidden_upper_usd.c4_political_substitution_note: CRITICAL DISTINCTION: The $5.3B transferred to Believe in People + CCKc4 501(c)(4)s IS NOT charitable giving under standard methodology. Flagged here as a disclosed-but-excluded figure for transparency. If published lists incorrectly classify c4 transfers as 'philanthropy,' they inflate Koch's giving ~5x the real observable number.
hidden_upper_usd.estate_plans_note: Koch's stated intent to leave 'most' of his wealth to Stand Together community + Believe in People is NOT charitable estate giving under the 501(c)(3) standard. Zero uplift — the signal is negative (wealth is being routed AWAY from traditional charity).
hidden_upper_usd.total_method: anonymous only. c4 political transfers EXPLICITLY EXCLUDED (not charity).

rollup.tier_rule: Observable 501(c)(3) giving ~$110M/yr averages close to the 10% annual benchmark for a $68B net-worth individual with 15% liquidity. On a narrow c3-only metric Koch looks borderline-compliant. However, the dominant story is the $5.3B c4 transfer — an order-of-magnitude larger vehicle that is NOT charity. Tier B is appropriate because: (a) observable c3 giving exists and is non-trivial (~$1.1B decade), (b) but Koch has DELIBERATELY routed the overwhelming majority of his giving-capable wealth into political advocacy vehicles, (c) he has NOT signed the Giving Pledge and is not plausibly on track to give 50%+ to charity.
rollup.tier_published_caveat: Readers should see Koch as the canonical political-substitution case. His 501(c)(3) foundation giving is real and tracked. But $5.3B to c4 political advocacy is ~5x all his lifetime charitable giving — a deliberate structural choice. Unlike Ellison (signed Giving Pledge, unfulfilled), Koch never promised and his legacy plan is explicitly c4-centric. Published-list classification MUST distinguish c3 giving from c4 transfers or it will misrepresent his philanthropic profile by 5-10x.
rollup.expected_5pct_tenure_method: 5% × net_worth_best × liquidity_pct × years_as_billionaire (uncapped tenure); computed by aggregate_v3.py

scrooge_legacy_score_internal_only.note: v2 formula continuity. Pledge breach = 0 because Koch never signed. Giving gap is moderate because observable c3 giving is non-trivial relative to expected. Red flag penalty saturates due to political substitution pattern. DO NOT PUBLISH this number — we publish tiers only.
